title: What is Optical Flow?
concept: Optical flow is the pattern of apparent motion of pixels between two consecutive frames, representing how objects move.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main structural bottleneck of 3D CNNs?

options.text: High memory consumption due to 3D activation volumes
They only work on static images

exercises.explain: Correct! 3D CNNs require storing huge volumes of activations across time, height, and width, making them computationally heavy.

cards.title: How do you see wind?
cards.body: You can't see wind directly. But you know it's blowing because you see leaves rustling, trash rolling, and branches bending. In video, we can't see 'motion' as a physical thing. We see it because pixels change positions! This pixel movement is called Optical Flow.

cards.title: Optical Flow
cards.body: Optical Flow is the pattern of apparent motion of objects, surfaces, and edges in a visual scene. It is calculated by tracking how individual pixels move from one video frame to the next.

cards.title: Finger-painting trails
cards.body: Imagine dipping your finger in wet paint and dragging it across a canvas. The trail left behind shows exactly where your finger went. Optical flow does this for every pixel in a video, leaving a virtual trail of where it moved.

cards.title: Tracking a ball
cards.body: If a red soccer ball moves from the left side of the screen to the right: the optical flow algorithm looks at the two frames, identifies the red pixels, and calculates that they moved 10 pixels to the right.

cards.title: Apparent vs. Real Motion
cards.body: Optical flow measures **apparent** motion, which isn't always real motion! If a light source moves across a static ball, the pixels change color, making it look like the ball is moving. Conversely, a rotating uniform sphere has no optical flow because its pixels don't change texture!

cards.title: Static vs. Motion Features
cards.body: Comparing static appearance analysis with optical flow motion analysis:

left.text:
Detects shape and color
e.g. Red sphere, white lines
Extracted from a single frame

right.text:
Detects velocity and direction
e.g. Moving right at 12 px/sec
Extracted from frame differences

exercises.prompt: What does optical flow measure in a video?

options.text: The apparent motion of pixels between consecutive frames
The depth of objects in a single photo
The audio volume of a video file
The chemical makeup of the camera lens

exercises.explain: Correct! Optical flow tracks pixel movements from one frame to the next.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A moving light casting a shadow on a completely static wall can create optical flow.
exercises.explain: True. Optical flow measures apparent motion. Since the shadow moves and changes pixel values, the algorithm registers it as motion, even if the wall is static.

exercises.sentence: Optical flow requires at least ___ consecutive frames to compute pixel motion.
exercises.explain: To measure movement, you need at least two frames: a starting position and an ending position.

exercises.prompt: Which of these scenarios will yield an optical flow of ZERO?

options.text: A static camera filming a completely still empty room
A person walking slowly across the camera field of view
A camera panning left to film a landscape

exercises.explain: If nothing is moving and the camera is static, no pixels change position, resulting in zero optical flow.

exercises.prompt: Why is optical flow highly valuable for video analysis models compared to raw pixel values?

options.text: It isolates the motion information, removing background distractions like lighting and static colors.
It automatically compresses video files into small ZIP archives.
It increases the number of color channels from 3 to 10.
It converts video frames into high-frequency audio vectors.

exercises.explain: Exactly! Optical flow isolates motion by highlighting only what moves and how fast, removing static background elements. Next: how do we represent this motion mathematically?
